title: Tiktok Censorship Algorithm and Expression
summary: A short study of tiktok's censorship algorithm and its effects on the userbase.
showNotes: Part of the Supernova podcast series where Master of Media Design students investigate algorithms embedded in our everyday lives.
transcript:
This social media has taken the world by storm. With approximately 1 billion users daily, TikTok is unavoidable. From a report from TikTok, approximately 1 billion users still use TikTok on a day-to-day basis.

However, that raises the question, with that many users and that many people using the app, how does one moderate such a platform? Now what we're going to talk about here isn't necessarily how the algorithm itself works. And while that would be interesting, I think it would be more fascinating to look into the way this censorship and this mediation of media on TikTok is perceived by the people. More specifically, it would be more interesting to look into the way the censorship on TikTok affects self-expression.

And even in minute ways, you will find that the way the algorithm shapes self-expression and identities is non-negligible. One idea is that censorship and filter algorithms are not just neutral tools. Instead, they actively decide what topics get amplified and what gets buried.

And those decisions often seem biased. Here is an account from Liz Mendez, a prolific designer studying a master's in media design at HEAD Geneva. And she uses TikTok often, and seems to have perceived this bias.

I know that they're very, very aware about the naked body, the part of the woman's body. People are censored for that. And I think that most of the time, it's when a woman wears something that we... For example, when we see the décolleté.

Another common suspicion is that there might even be human intervention involved in these algorithms. People see the bias and assume that someone, somewhere, must be pulling the strings. Another account from Karim, a TikToker and content creator, as well as social media manager, living in Cologne, Germany.

There should be some sort of person or way to verify if this information is okay or not. Like, there has to be some kind of process to check into those censored videos. To see what exactly should be okay on this platform and what not.

Now on both of those accounts, you can notice that they express a form of bias within TikTok and the way that it moderates the app. Some videos that are, from my perspective, really harmless, get censored because of one wrong wording. And while they do recognize and notice that there is a bias in the way that TikTok moderates and shows this content, they also seem to use TikTok as a source of information, as a source of news.

I know that I'm always aware of what happens during the time thanks to TikTok. And sometimes I'm aware faster than my friends or my family. I mean, I've talked a lot about, like, searching up information and news and stuff like that, but TikTok is also an entertainment platform.

They both seem to praise TikTok as a source of unbiased and quick information. But they still notice that there is a bias in the way that it is moderated and in the way the information is brought to them. Now I find it more interesting to notice that they seem to perceive the information that is brought to them as unbiased and they feel empowered in doing so.

It makes me wonder how many other people feel that way and how dangerous that could be. Now beyond what is removed, there is also the question of self-censorship. When people anticipate their content might be flagged or suppressed, they often adjust what they say or how they say it.

As one article put it, this may prevent users from expressing themselves in an authentic manner as they are prompted to align what they express with the commercial goals of the platform. Quote from Unglis Markle et al., experiences of censorship on TikTok across marginalized identities. Now Karim, which I have interviewed, also expressed the same feelings.

Almost every time I think about posting something or think about an idea, I always also have to think like, okay, is this even reaching anyone? Is this getting like under the radar or even blocked or censored? Now the experiences we've talked about today along with the research paint a picture of a system that is complex, inconsistent and sometimes deeply unfair. But this case study is small and narrow and there is so much more to learn. Now the people that use the app seem to say that TikTok is biased in the way that it censors and it does censor a specific type of people, but is it an active effort from the platform? That remains to be seen.

It is impossible within the scope of this podcast to state an opinion on this matter. However, I will leave you with this. Have you ever felt like your voice was being drowned out or like you had to change what you had to say to fit in? These are questions we all need to ask.

But speaking of being drowned out, this music is pretty loud. Let's see what Shazam has to say. Coming up, Bruno Acosta, a software engineer and a professional when it comes to Shazam.
